html:
The Recoup Health School Program

The Recoup Health School Program aims to revolutionize student wellness in India through a pioneering, holistic approach to school-based healthcare. Under the visionary tagline "Future Solutions Now," our initiative proactively addresses the growing epidemic of physical, mental, and emotional health issues among school-going children. Rooted in scientific rigor and compassionate care, Recoup Health (RH) will deliver age-appropriate, multidisciplinary interventions, enhancing not just student health but academic outcomes and lifelong well-being.

Beyond Just Academics: Prioritizing Health & Wellness

Schools have the power to shape not just minds, but lifelong habits. A strong foundation in health and wellness is crucial to unlocking a student's full potential—academically, physically, and emotionally. Numerous studies have shown that healthy students are better learners, and school-based health promotion significantly reduces absenteeism, improves cognitive function, and fosters overall well-being. (Basch CE. "Healthier students are better learners." J School Health, 2011)

From repeated illness, allergies and nutritional deficiencies to musculoskeletal pain, digital fatigue, learning disorders, and mental health struggles—children today face a wide spectrum of health issues that demand a preventive, proactive and holistic approach.

According to the CDC, more than one-third of high school students do not engage in the recommended levels of physical activity whereas Regular exercise is essential for bone and muscle health, weight regulation, and cognitive functioning. (CDC. "Youth Risk Behavior Survey," 2019)

Compelling evidence shows that skipping breakfast negatively affects learning. Even mild malnutrition in early life can impair cognitive development and academic achievement. (Pollitt E. "Breakfast and cognition: an integrative summary." Am J Clin Nutr, 1995)

Indian children face a rising epidemic: 9–27.5% are overweight, and 1–12.9% are obese (World Health Organization (India). "Childhood Overweight and Obesity," 2011). Overweight youth are more likely to carry metabolic risk factors—hypertension, insulin resistance, and cholesterol abnormalities—into adulthood, predisposing them to diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, and arthritis.

More than half of school children experience musculoskeletal pain, largely driven by poor posture, heavy school bags, and prolonged screen time. Skoffer B. (Low back pain in 15–16-year-old children associated with poor posture." BMC Musculoskeletal Disorders, 2007) The situation has worsened significantly since the COVID-19 lockdowns. Left unaddressed, these issues often continue into adulthood, increasing the risk of chronic pain and long-term disability.

An estimated 10–15% of school-aged children struggle with learning disabilities, many of which remain undiagnosed or unsupported, leading to academic failure and low self-esteem. (American Academy of Pediatrics. "Identifying and Treating Learning Disabilities," 2011)

Recognizing this, RH has designed a comprehensive, integrative and multidisciplinary School Health Program that addresses the pressing and often overlooked health challenges that hinder students' academic performance and development.

By equipping students and their teachers with accurate health knowledge, practical life skills, and empowering habits, we aim to foster lifelong wellness and decision-making competence. Early education and structured intervention can prevent chronic diseases, reduce risk behaviours, and nurture physically, emotionally, and socially resilient young individuals.

The Building Blocks of a Better Future - The Key Components

As we know, healthy students learn better. RHSP covers essential areas of student wellness — ensuring a healthier future starts now.

  • Ergonomics and Musculoskeletal Disorder Prevention
  • Sports Injury Risk Reduction
  • Addressing Learning Challenges and Disabilities
  • Speech Sound and Articulation Issues
  • Communication and Language Disorders
  • Fluency and Stuttering Disorders
  • Obesity and Nutrition-Related Conditions
  • Chronic Disease Prevention Strategies
  • Mental Health: Managing Stress, Enhancing Emotional Well-being, Fostering Healthy Relationships, Promoting Core Values, Gender Sensitivity, Conduct Regulation, and Behavioural Interventions
  • Neurodevelopmental Repatterning through Coherence-Building Techniques
  • Reproductive and Sexual Health Education
  • Ensuring Safety from Violence and Encouraging Responsible Digital Behavior
  • Preventing Overuse of Digital Media
  • Substance Use Prevention and Management
  • First Aid and Emergency Response Skills
  • Capacity-Building Program for Educators

Beyond the Blackboard: RHSP Health Solutions for Schools

Integrating health care with education, we help schools become spaces of healing, learning, and thriving. And thus, our services include:

  1. Preventive Screening & Health Evaluations at School
    Regular, structured health assessments to identify physical, psychological, and developmental issues early.
  2. Interactive Health Education Workshops
    Lecture demonstrations for students, parents, and teachers—held during events like PTMs or Health Weeks—cover topics like posture, nutrition, screen hygiene, puberty, and mental well-being.
  3. Teacher Training for Early Identification
    Empowering class teachers to spot early signs of:
    • Learning disabilities (e.g., dyslexia, ADHD)
    • Postural and movement-related disorders
    • Speech and communication difficulties (e.g., stammering, articulation errors)
    • Behavioral and emotional concerns
  4. On-Site or Referred Specialist Consultations
    Access to a multidisciplinary team, including physiotherapists, psychologists, speech-language pathologists, pediatricians, nutritionists, and developmental specialists for at-risk students.
  5. Mental Health & Emotional Wellbeing Support
    Screening and counselling services to address anxiety, stress, peer pressure, and emotional challenges, through one-on-one sessions and group activities.
  6. Customized Health Reports for Parents - INTRODUCING RSHS - Recoup School Health Score
    Individual health summaries with recommendations and follow-ups to keep families informed and engaged in their child's well-being.
  7. Nutrition & Lifestyle Counselling
    Sessions promoting healthy eating habits, physical activity, and sleep hygiene are tailored to each age group.
  8. Physical Fitness & Posture Correction Modules
    Structured group activities, ergonomics awareness, and corrective exercise plans to prevent long-term musculoskeletal issues.
  9. Vision, Dental, and Hearing Camps
    Periodic checks by specialists to catch common but often overlooked issues affecting learning and attention.
  10. Health Awareness Campaigns & Competitions
    Interactive events like poster contests, quizzes, and health clubs to embed wellness into school culture.
  11. Parent Engagement & Support Forums
    Regular talks, Q&A sessions, and support groups help parents navigate child development and health challenges.
  12. School Health Portal (Optional Digital Integration) - PHASE 2
    A dedicated digital platform for health records, appointment scheduling, educational content, and teleconsultations—making health access easier and data-driven.

A Whole-Child Approach, Backed by Whole-Team Expertise

As they say, it takes a village to raise a child, but a team to keep them thriving. Behind every student's health journey is a powerhouse team of specialists working together to prevent, identify, and transform health challenges into growth opportunities.

Our interdisciplinary team brings unmatched depth, experience, and compassion to ensure holistic care for every child.

RECOUP School Health Team Includes

  • Pediatric Orthopaedic Surgeon
  • Rehabilitation Physician
  • Functional Medicine Physician
  • Lifestyle Medicine Physician
  • Sports Medicine Physician
  • Ayurveda Physician
  • Naturopathy and Yoga Physician
  • Other Specialist Physicians (ENT, Dermatology, Endocrinology, etc.)
  • Speech Language Pathologist
  • Occupational Therapist
  • Physiotherapist
  • Clinical Psychologist
  • Nutritionist
  • Nurse
  • Additional experts, brought in as needed for comprehensive care

Introducing RSHS - Recoup School Health Score

Like academic report cards track learning, RSHS tracks the child's health and well-being in a clear, interactive, and easy-to-understand format. It's not just a score; it's a roadmap to raising healthy, happy, and high-performing students.

This gives parents and schools powerful insights into every child's physical, emotional, and preventive health—all in one place.

Key Components include:

  1. Student Details & Summary
    • Basic Information: Name, Age, Grade, School Name, Date of Assessment
    • Health Score (0–100):
      • 80–100: Excellent – Healthy and Active
      • 60–79: Moderate – Some improvements needed
      • Below 60: Needs Attention – High-risk areas detected
    • Key Takeaways:
      • 3–5 personalized insights or recommendations (e.g., "Needs better posture awareness", "Slight vitamin D deficiency detected")
  2. Physical Health Assessment
    • Growth and Development: Height, Weight, BMI, Growth Percentile (compared to age group)
    • Posture and Musculoskeletal Health: Postural screening (e.g., scoliosis- Adam's Test, forward head posture) and Muscle strength and flexibility tests including Beighton's score
    • Cardiovascular Fitness: Resting heart rate, Aerobic endurance and Physical activity level
    • Nutrition and Metabolism: Dietary habits and meal quality, hydration, Risk of vitamin and mineral deficiencies (through NPE)
    • Puberty and Hormonal Health (for girls): Early or delayed signs of puberty, menstrual health concerns, and awareness
  3. Mental and Emotional Well-being
    • Stress and Anxiety Levels: Self-assessment of emotional stress and school-life balance
    • Sleep and Energy Levels: Sleep duration and quality, signs of fatigue, focus issues
    • Screen Time and Digital Habits: Duration, patterns, and impact on behavior and mood
    • Neurodevelopmental and Learning Assessment: Screening for Learning Difficulties (LD), Red flags for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), Indicators of Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD), School-readiness and classroom behavior markers
    • Speech and Communication Disorders: Evaluation for Speech Sound Disorders, Identification of Stammering, Fluency, and Language Delays
  4. Preventive Health Risks
    • Vision and Eye Health: Signs of eye strain or myopia, eye check-up (Snellen and Jaegar Chart), Color blindness (Ishihara Card)
    • Immunity and Common Illness Risk: Frequency of infections, recommendations for immunity support, Vaccine boosters
    • Lifestyle Risk Factors: Sedentary habits, junk food consumption, hydration status
  5. Personalized Action Plan
    • Health Recommendations (based on assessment):
      1. Diet Modifications (e.g., increase fruits or reduce sugary snacks)
      2. Exercise Suggestions (e.g., 15 minutes of stretching daily)
      3. Mental Health Tips (e.g., mindfulness exercises, journaling)
    • Follow-Up Suggestions:
      1. Immediate action required? (Referral to doctor, further testing)
      2. Next screening date for review and progress tracking
  6. School Health Summary (For Administrators)
    • Average Health Score: By grade or section
    • Common Health Issues: Trends observed across student groups (e.g., postural issues in 40% of Grade 6)
    • School-Wide Recommendations: Suggestions for integrating wellness into the school environment (e.g., ergonomic furniture, activity breaks, mental health workshops, healthier meals etc.)

Health can't be an afterthought in today's hyperconnected, high-pressure world. It must be part of the school experience, as essential as math or science. With RSHS, schools can create a culture of care, while parents stay empowered with real, actionable insights.

How We Win: Spreading the School Health Revolution

Empowering institutions, energizing students, and engaging communities—one school at a time.

We don't just introduce a health program—we deliver a movement. Here's how we create impact, build trust, and scale our reach:

  1. Direct Outreach:
    • Call & Email School Leaders
      Get directly in touch with principals, health coordinators, and PE teachers. It is important to pitch a clear value like healthier students, better attendance, improved academic performance.
    • Tailored Email Campaigns
      Create personalized pitches that speak to the school's needs and show how our program aligns with their values.
    • Meet and Offer a Free Consultation or Demo
      Show them what success looks like. A 30-minute session can spark a long-term partnership.
  2. Partnerships & Collaborations:
    • Tie-Up with Leading School Chains
      Collaborate with school networks like DPS, NPS, or private school groups to reach hundreds of schools through one partnership.
    • Working with Parent-Teacher Associations (PTAs)
      Winning the hearts of parents—our strongest ambassadors. A PTA endorsement can fast-track approvals.
    • Government, NGO Alliances and Disability Departments
      Aligning with state education boards, CSR programs, and wellness-focused NGOs to broaden our reach and gain institutional credibility.
  3. Workshops & Events: Create Buzz and Build Trust
    • Expert-Led Seminars and Webinars
      Hosting trendy and engaging sessions on nutrition, screen-time balance, fitness, and emotional wellness—open to students, teachers, and parents.
    • School Wellness Days
      Organize health fairs, fitness challenges, and student health screenings. A fun, visible way to show our value.
    • Competitions & Awareness Drives
      Launching poster-making contests, health quizzes, or classroom challenges that turn education into excitement.
  4. Digital & Social Media Campaigns
    • LinkedIn & Meta (Facebook/Instagram) Ads
      Targeting decision-makers—school admins, education consultants, and wellness advocates.
    • Testimonial Videos & Case Studies
      Sharing real-world transformations—before and after stories that make your value tangible.
    • WhatsApp & Telegram Channels
      Connecting with educators, school coordinators, and parent groups where they already communicate.
  5. Referral & Recognition Programs:
    • School Referral Program
      Rewarding schools that refer others with discounts, free screenings, or training sessions.
    • Certificates, Awards & Public Recognition
      Celebrate schools that adopt the program—through "Healthy School Awards" or "Wellness Leader" badges that boost their reputation. Involve media and politicians.

RHSP Revenue Strategy - Building a Scalable, Sustainable Model

  1. Subscription-Based Revenue: Predictable, Scalable, Recurring

    A. School-Wide Subscription (Annual Fee per Student): can have tiered Pricing by School Size

    1. Small Schools (up to 500 students): ₹X per student
    2. Medium Schools (500–1000 students): ₹Y per student
    3. Large Schools (1000+ students): ₹Z per student

    -Includes annual screenings, RSHS with standard AI reports, workshops, app access (PHASE 2), and regular consults - PPMs.

    B. Individual Enrollment Model

    -Allows per-student sign-up for schools not opting for full coverage.

    -Flexible pricing for parents directly enrolling their child.

  2. Service-Based Revenue: High-Impact, Transactional

    A. Health Screenings & Assessments

    -Standard & Comprehensive Check-Ups (priced per student) - by Specialist doctors

    -Optional add-ons for in-depth assessments, blood tests, biometrics, posture analysis etc.

    B. Workshops & Teacher Training

    -Nutrition, posture, digital wellness, mental health, and more

    -Priced per session or bundled annually

    C. Personalized Care Plans

    -Tailored reports and intervention packages

    -Follow-ups and specialist consultations charged separately

  3. Digital & Tech Revenue: Scalable Growth Channel - PHASE 2

    A. Dedicated Health App

    • School-wide licensing or direct-to-parent subscriptions
    • Premium features: AI-driven tips, habit tracking, gamified challenges

    B. Virtual Consultations

    • On-demand telemedicine support
    • Add-on services for parents, school nurses, and teachers
  4. CSR & Government Engagement: Strategic Partnerships

    A. CSR Sponsorships

    • Corporate funding for under-resourced schools
    • Reporting and impact dashboards for sponsors

    B. Government Wellness Programs

    • Integration into public health and education schemes
    • Ideal for district or state-level rollout
  5. Brand Collaborations & Products: Additional Revenue Streams

    A. School-Friendly Health Kits

    • Ergonomic supplies and furniture, hygiene tools, educational guides

    B. Smart Health Products - principally for sports

    • Posture wearables, fitness bands, app-linked trackers

Revenue Optimization Hacks

  • Bulk discounts for large institutions
  • Referral and early-bird incentives
  • Cross-selling app subscriptions and specialist consultations like developmental pediatrician, neurologist, endocrinologist, gynaecologist, etc.
  • Seasonal wellness drives to boost participation

A closer look at the Recoup Advantage

When students understand their bodies and minds, they thrive. We plant the seeds of awareness, confidence, and self-care early on, and here's how we can make it happen.

1. Unlocking Language, Unlocking Potential - SLP's role

Communication is the foundation of connection, learning, and confidence. Struggles with speech and language often hide beneath the surface—until they impact reading, writing, and social connection. Early intervention isn't just about speaking clearly—it's about building a child's capacity to succeed in every corner of school life. We help students speak clearly, connect confidently, and succeed completely, whether it's stuttering, articulation, or expressive language delays. From the playground to the classroom, our speech-language pathologists work to prevent, assess, diagnose, and treat a wide range of communication disorders—supporting students where it matters most.

Why does it matter?

Communication is the heartbeat of a child's learning, development, and social success. When children struggle to express themselves, understand others, or participate in classroom discussions, their confidence and academic progress can take a hit. That's where our Speech and Language Services step in—bringing expert support right into the school environment, so no child falls behind due to unseen communication barriers.

What are Speech and Language Pathologists (SLP's)?

Speech-Language Pathologists (SLPs) are highly trained professionals who work with children to prevent, identify, assess, diagnose, and treat a wide range of challenges related to:

  • Speech: How children produce sounds and words
  • Language: Understanding and using words to communicate
  • Social Communication: Using language to interact appropriately with peers and adults
  • Cognitive Communication: Memory, attention, problem-solving, and reasoning
  • Voice and Fluency: Stuttering, hoarseness, or inappropriate volume
  • Feeding and Swallowing: Difficulties with eating safely and efficiently

Whether it's helping a child speak more clearly, participate in class discussions, or feel confident reading aloud, SLPs provide targeted therapy and work closely with teachers, parents, and support staff.

Common Communication Challenges

  1. Speech Sound Disorders
    • Have difficulty pronouncing certain sounds (e.g., "r" or "th")
    • Speak in ways that are hard to understand
    • Omit or substitute sounds ("nake" for "snake")
  2. Language and Literacy Challenges
    • Struggle to understand what they hear
    • Have difficulty following instructions or answering questions
    • Use short or incomplete sentences
    • Face delays in reading and writing
  3. Cognitive Communication Issues - may be linked to memory, organization and problem solving due to:
    • Learning disabilities
    • Brain injury
    • Developmental delays
    They can affect both academic tasks and daily communication.
  4. Social Communication Issues
    • Finding hard to start or maintain conversations
    • Missing social cues or facial expressions
    • Struggle to make and keep friends
    This is especially relevant in children with Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD) or social anxiety.
  5. Stuttering
    • Repeating words or sounds
    • Long pauses or blocks when speaking
    • Avoiding speaking situations
    Stuttering can make a child feel isolated or anxious in class.
  6. Voice Disorders
    • Sounding hoarse or breathy
    • Speaking too loudly or softly
    • Sounding nasal or monotone
    Voice issues can interfere with their confidence and classroom participation
  7. Feeding and Swallowing Issues
    • Coughing or gagging while eating
    • Avoiding certain textures
    • Struggling with chewing or swallowing
    These issues can affect nutrition, growth, and classroom energy levels.

Screening & Early Intervention: The Key to Change

Early identification of speech and language concerns is crucial.

Our SLPs conduct school-based screenings to detect challenges before they become roadblocks. These check-ups are quick, effective, and can set the stage for timely support through individual or group therapy.

Empowering Parents and Teachers

We believe that communication support doesn't stop at the therapist's door.

Our team regularly conducts:

  • Workshops for teachers on classroom communication strategies
  • Training sessions for parents to encourage speech and language development at home

Together, we create a support ecosystem that surrounds each child.

SLPs and the IEP: Customizing Support for Every Child

An Individualized Education Plan (IEP) is a legal document that outlines the unique learning needs and support strategies for a child with a disability.

SLPs play a critical role in designing and implementing the communication-related goals of a child's IEP, such as:

  • Improving expressive and receptive language
  • Enhancing social communication in group settings
  • Strengthening fluency or articulation

They also help design educational accommodations (such as visual aids or extra response time) to ensure that each child has an equal opportunity to succeed.

A Typical IEP Includes:

  • The child's current communication and academic performance
  • Measurable goals for improvement
  • Related services (like speech therapy) that the child qualifies for
  • Modifications or accommodations needed for success
  • A timeline to monitor progress

In Summary: Giving Voice to Potential

Through expert intervention, early screenings, tailored therapy, and close collaboration with school staff and families, Speech-Language Pathologists are key players in helping every child find their voice—and use it confidently in every part of their life.

2. RHSP Occupational Therapy (OT) Program - Helping Children Do What Children Need to Do!

Designed to support the development and well-being of students by enhancing their ability to participate meaningfully in school-based activities, Occupational therapists (OTs) are uniquely trained to address the physical, cognitive, psychosocial, and sensory aspects of student functioning that impact learning, self-care, play, and social engagement.

The services are integrated within the educational environment, focusing on enabling students to achieve their academic and developmental goals, participate fully in school life, and transition effectively through different stages of education.

Who are OT Practitioners?

Occupational therapists are allied health professionals with expertise in activity analysis, environmental modification, and the development of functional skills. In a school setting, they work collaboratively with educators, parents, and other specialists to support student participation across multiple domains, including:

  • Academic performance
  • Play and leisure
  • Social interaction
  • Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
  • Pre-vocational and transitional skills

Key Components of RHSP OT Program:

  1. Adaptation of Activities and Environments
    OTs assess and recommend environmental modifications that enhance participation. This may include adjusting classroom seating, modifying playground settings, or providing strategies to support inclusion in physical education, art, and music classes.
  2. Promoting Independence in Daily Living Skills
    Students are supported in developing essential self-care and organisational abilities such as personal hygiene, time management, and use of materials. OTs may also guide school staff in making adjustments to restroom or classroom layouts to improve accessibility and independence.
  3. Assessment and Recommendation of Assistive Technology
    Occupational therapists evaluate the need for adaptive tools to facilitate learning and participation. This includes recommending items such as pencil grips, slant boards, ergonomic seating, or digital devices to support written expression or communication.
  4. Instructional Collaboration with Teachers
    OTs collaborate with teachers to integrate supportive strategies into classroom routines. These include structured movement breaks, visual schedules, alternative task approaches, and universal design techniques that benefit all students.
  5. Addressing Sensory-Motor Needs
    Students with sensory processing challenges may struggle with attention, regulation, and classroom engagement. Occupational therapists implement targeted interventions to improve body awareness, coordination, and sensory integration through strength-building, movement-based, and perceptual-motor activities.
  6. Supporting Social, Emotional, and Communication Skills
    OT services may include training in emotional regulation, coping strategies, and social skills. These interventions enable students to navigate peer interactions, follow routines, and manage classroom expectations more effectively.
  7. Family and Caregiver Engagement
    Parental involvement is a critical component of student success. OTs provide families with home-based strategies, caregiver education, and counselling to reinforce skill development beyond the school setting.

Program Objectives

  • Enhance participation and engagement in school activities
  • Support developmentally appropriate independence
  • Facilitate access to curriculum and inclusive education
  • Foster collaboration between school teams and families
  • Promote long-term functional outcomes for students with diverse needs

At Recoup Health, we believe that every child deserves to feel capable, connected, and confident—whether they're tying a shoelace, writing a sentence, or sharing a toy.

3. Behavioral Health Services and Health Education

Helping Students Thrive—Emotionally, Mentally, and Socially

What if your classroom was a place where every student not only learns but also feels supported, heard, and empowered? That's what the RHSP Behavioral Health and Wellness Program is all about.

We bring together a team of experts— Functional medicine physicians, Rehabilitation physicians, occupational and behaviour therapists, clinical psychologists, clinical nutritionists, counselors, and educators—to make sure students have everything they need to be emotionally resilient, socially confident, and mentally strong.

Key Areas of Focus

  1. Mental and Emotional Well-being
    • Stress Management & Resilience Training: Equipping students with coping strategies to manage daily stress, academic pressures, and life transitions.
    • Common Conditions Addressed:
      • Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD
      • Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD)
      • Anxiety and Depression
      • Performance Anxiety and Exam Stress
      • Risk-taking Behaviors and Aggression
      • Body Image Concerns and Body Dysmorphic Disorders
      • Gender Dysphoria and Identity Support

    Support is delivered through one-on-one counseling, classroom-based interventions, and teacher and parent training sessions to ensure a continuum of care.

  2. Nutrition, Eating Habits, and Lifestyle Education
    • Addressing nutritional imbalances, including undernutrition, obesity, and disordered eating habits (e.g., binge eating, anorexia).
    • Promoting healthy eating behaviors through child-friendly nutrition education and behavior-based counselling.
    • Creating awareness on the link between diet, cognition, emotional health, and academic performance.
  3. Sleep Hygiene and Physical Activity
    • Educating students about the importance of adequate sleep for growth, mental clarity, emotional regulation, and behavior.
    • Encouraging daily physical activity as a tool for stress relief, emotional stability, and cognitive enhancement.
    • Partnering with physical educators and parents to create movement-friendly routines at school and home.

    Through games and group activities, exploring the link between screen time, sleep quality, and school performance—and we help kids build a sleep routine that works.

  4. Substance Use Awareness and Prevention
    • Evidence-based modules on the risks of tobacco, alcohol, and recreational drug use, including the impact on brain development, decision-making, and peer relationships.
    • Interactive workshops, visual aids, and peer-led discussions to build awareness and resistance skills in a safe, non-judgmental environment.
  5. Positive Behavior Development & Social Skills Training
    • Programs are designed to enhance self-awareness, empathy, kindness, teamwork, emotional regulation, and peer support.
    • Age-appropriate sessions that include topics such as consent, healthy relationships, self-esteem, and navigating peer pressure.

Program Highlights

  • Individual and Group counselling services are delivered by clinical psychologists and behavioral health professionals.
  • Parent and Teacher Workshops on recognizing behavioral signs, early intervention strategies, and promoting healthy environments.
  • Classroom Health Modules customized to age and developmental level, integrating experiential learning, games, and storytelling.
  • Monitoring and Referral System for children needing specialized interventions in partnership with pediatric and adolescent mental health experts.

Our Commitment

At RECOUP, we believe that behavioral health is foundational to academic success and lifelong wellness. Our school-based behavioral health and education services are built on a foundation of empathy, science, and collaboration—ensuring that every child has the tools to flourish emotionally, socially, and intellectually.

4. Reproductive Health Education for Adolescent Girls

Empowering adolescent girls with knowledge, confidence, and support

Adolescence is a critical period of physical, emotional, and social transformation. One of the most essential aspects of this transition for girls is understanding reproductive health. RHSP offers a structured, age-appropriate, and evidence-based approach to educating young girls about their reproductive systems, menstrual health, and emotional well-being—empowering them to make informed choices about their bodies and lives.

Menstrual Health

  • Menstrual Cycle Awareness: Understanding biology and hormonal changes.
  • Hygiene & Products: Choosing safe menstrual products (pads, tampons, cups), hygiene practices, and disposal methods.
  • Common Concerns:
    • Dysmenorrhea (painful periods)
    • PMS (mood changes, fatigue)
    • Irregular/heavy bleeding
    • PCOS – signs, implications, management
    • White discharge – normal vs concerning

Sexual Health & Contraception

  • Understanding Sexual Activity: Consent, readiness, peer pressure.
  • Contraceptive Education: Types, safe practices, myth-busting.
  • STIs & HPV:
    • Awareness, prevention, treatment
    • HPV vaccination – importance and availability

Emotional & Psychological Support

Common Issues:

  • Eating disorders, body image issues
  • PMS-related mood swings
  • Sleep disturbances
  • Stress, anxiety, depression

Support Mechanisms:

  • Individual counseling
  • Peer support
  • Mental health awareness and referrals

Program Approach & Delivery

  • Interactive Workshops and Group Discussions
  • One-on-One Counseling and Confidential Support Services
  • Parent-Teacher Education Modules to build supportive ecosystems
  • Collaborative Goal Setting for menstrual tracking, emotional regulation, and hygiene practices
  • Referral and Escalation Pathways for students needing advanced medical or psychological interventions

5. RH SPRINT (School Program for Resilience, Injury Prevention, and Training)

"Because every school has the potential to create the next Tendulkar, Bindra, Sania, or Sindhu."

RH SPRINT is a comprehensive, evidence-based initiative designed to identify, support, and enhance athletic potential in school-aged children. The program emphasizes injury prevention, physical and biomechanical assessments, and long-term athletic development — fostering a culture of safety, excellence, and high performance from an early age.

Sports Injury Prevention and Performance Optimization

Our multidisciplinary approach focuses on preventing injuries and enhancing sports performance through the following key components:

  • Pre-Participation Evaluation
    A detailed assessment of the student's physical readiness for sports participation, identifying any pre-existing conditions or risk factors.
  • Ergonomics in Sports Gear Selection
    Guidance on the appropriate selection and use of sport-specific gear, footwear, and protective equipment to minimize injury risks and improve function.
  • Biomechanical Analysis and Technique Correction
    Evaluation of posture, movement patterns, and sport-specific techniques, followed by remediation strategies to optimize performance and reduce strain or injury.
  • Sport-Specific Injury Prevention Protocols
    Implementation of structured injury prevention programs, such as the FIFA 11+ warm-up program for football players, and similar evidence-based modules tailored to other sports.

Comprehensive Physical Screening for Young Athletes (Ages 7+)

Targeted at children engaged in moderate to intensive sports activities or competitive athletics, this screening provides early detection of physical, nutritional, and biomechanical concerns.

  1. Laboratory Testing
    • Hemoglobin and Ferritin (to assess anemia and iron stores)
    • Urine Routine (to detect infections and hydration status)
    • Vitamin D, Vitamin B12, Calcium, Magnesium, Zinc (to evaluate micronutrient adequacy)
  2. Pulmonary Function
    • Spirometry to measure lung capacity and respiratory efficiency
  3. Nutritional Assessment
    • Evaluation of dietary intake, hydration status, and caloric needs
    • Analysis of micronutrient sufficiency for athletic performance
  4. Body Composition
    • Assessment of body fat percentage, muscle mass, and lean body mass
  5. Cardiovascular Fitness
    • Heart Rate Variability (HRV) to evaluate recovery potential and autonomic nervous system balance
  6. Bone Health
    • Bone Mineral Density (BMD) testing to monitor skeletal development and prevent stress fractures
  7. Biomechanics and Gait
    • Comprehensive musculoskeletal screening
    • 2D foot scanning, gait analysis, and evaluation of foot mechanics
  8. Custom Orthotics
    • Prescription of personalized orthotic devices to optimize alignment, reduce injury risk, and enhance performance

Program Outcomes

  • Early identification of physical vulnerabilities and nutritional deficiencies
  • Enhanced motor control, agility, and coordination
  • Improved sports performance through customized corrective strategies
  • Reduced incidence of injuries and faster return-to-play for young athletes
  • Promotion of long-term well-being, athletic success, and resilience

6. RHSP Digital Balance: Promoting Healthy Media Use and Well-being in Schools

  1. Addressing Internet and Mobile Phone Addiction
    • Identification and early intervention for students showing signs of compulsive digital media use.
    • Understanding patterns of overuse that may affect academic performance, sleep, and interpersonal relationships.
  2. Peer-to-Peer Engagement
    • Implementation of peer-led initiatives to foster open conversations around responsible digital usage.
    • Promoting mutual support and shared learning through relatable experiences and collective problem-solving.
  3. Skill-Based, Competency Enhancement Approach
    • Focus on building essential psychosocial competencies such as:
      • Critical thinking and media literacy
      • Self-monitoring and self-reflection
      • Emotional regulation and decision-making
      • Communication and interpersonal skills
    • Encouraging students to reflect on their own digital habits and make informed, mindful choices.
  4. Mitigating Negative Psychosocial Impacts
    • Strategies to reduce comorbid symptoms such as anxiety, depression, and school-related challenges like procrastination and disengagement.
    • Providing psychological support to students at risk.
  5. Awareness and Information Dissemination
    • Conducting workshops, campaigns, and educational sessions to raise awareness about the risks of overuse and the benefits of digital balance.
    • Promoting safe, purposeful, and time-bound use of digital platforms.
  6. Integration of Positive Psychology Principles
    • Fostering self-efficacy, resilience, and positive self-concept through strengths-based interventions.
    • Encouraging students to build a fulfilling offline life, anchored in hobbies, social connections, and physical activity.

7. RHSP Teachers' Training Program: Empowering Educators for Student Success

RHSP - TTP is designed to equip educators with the tools, knowledge, and strategies to enhance students' academic and personal growth. Through a comprehensive curriculum, this program aims to address key areas of student development and promote a more effective, inclusive, and supportive classroom environment. Below are the core objectives and topics covered in the training:

Handwriting and Fine Motor Skills

  • Understanding the normal progression of writing skills.
  • Identifying issues such as poor pencil grip, letter formation, and visual perception skills.
  • The importance of posture while writing.
  • Activities designed to improve handwriting legibility.

Concentration

  • Identifying signs of concentration difficulties.
  • Developing sensory-based strategies to support focus.
  • Classroom activities designed to boost student attention and engagement.

Movement and Coordination

  • Recognizing signs of poor coordination in children (both upper and lower limbs).
  • Understanding the underlying systems and skills necessary for successful coordination.
  • Practical activities to support children with coordination difficulties.

Organization and Planning

  • Identifying students with poor organizational and planning abilities.
  • Implementing strategies to help children develop these essential executive skills.
  • Understanding the broader context of executive functioning for both students and teachers.

Techniques for Supporting Emotional and Behavioral Regulation

  • Mindfulness Practices: Introduction to simple mindfulness exercises, such as focusing on breath or body sensations.
  • Social-Emotional Learning (SEL): Utilizing evidence-based SEL programs to enhance students' emotional intelligence.

6. Substance Abuse Training

  • Identifying Signs and Symptoms: Recognizing changes in behavior, mood, appearance, and academic performance.
  • Understanding Substances: Learning about the effects, risks, and red flags associated with drugs and alcohol.
  • Communication Skills: Effective ways to communicate with students, parents, and colleagues about substance use issues.
  • Referral Resources: Knowing where to refer students for help and support.
  • Self-Care Strategies: Tools to help teachers manage stress and prevent burnout.

Benefits:

  • Empowered Teachers: Teachers will be equipped to spot early signs of substance abuse and provide support.
  • Early Intervention: Improved outcomes for students struggling with substance abuse through timely support.
  • Prevention: Raising awareness and promoting healthy choices within the student community.

Teaching Methodologies for Efficient Learning

  • Creating a Positive Learning Environment: Techniques for fostering a safe, supportive, and respectful classroom atmosphere.
  • Building Relationships with Students: Encouraging teachers to show genuine interest in students' lives and learning journeys.
  • Collaborating with Educators: Promoting teamwork among educators to share best practices and continuously improve teaching methods.

Autism, Sensory System, and Sensory Processing Disorders

  • Sensory System Basics: Understanding how children process sensory input.
  • Autism & SPD: Identifying sensory processing challenges such as hypersensitivity or hyposensitivity.
  • Recognizing Sensory Issues: Practical signs like avoiding textures or overstimulation.
  • Strategies for Sensory Regulation: Tools like fidget items, sensory breaks, and classroom modifications.
  • Classroom Integration: Creating sensory-friendly zones to support students with sensory challenges.

First Responder Training and Certification

  • Emergency Medical Response: CPR, choking assistance, and AED use.
  • Injury Prevention & First Aid: Managing common school injuries and accidents.
  • Customized Training: Tailored sessions for coaches (sports injuries) and teaching staff (classroom injuries).
  • Hands-On Practice: In-person demonstrations and skill application.
  • Emergency Preparedness: Handling evacuations, fire drills, and other non-medical crises.
  • Certification: Teachers receive certification upon course completion.
